5th Grade · Ms. Jessica · Week of April 20–24, 2026
Ms. Jessica · Term 4 · 2026
Classroom Newsletter How We Organize Ourselves
📅 Week of April 20–24, 2026
✦ New Unit: How We Organize Ourselves
🎉

Our final IB/PYP unit begins this Monday!

After a remarkable Exhibition journey, we are ready for a brand-new inquiry. This week we launch How We Organize Ourselves, exploring how systems of government, laws, and community shape the way people live, protect one another, and respond when those systems are threatened. It's going to be a powerful and meaningful finish to 5th grade!

🏛️
UOI — Social Studies
U.S. Government · Constitution · Civic Participation
▾

Our central inquiry question: How do organized systems shape the way communities live, protect one another, and what responsibilities do individuals hold within them?

🇺🇸

This week students dive into the U.S. Constitution and the three branches of government (Legislative, Executive, and Judicial). We'll explore how the system of checks and balances was designed specifically to prevent any one branch from holding too much power. Key question: What would happen if one branch had all the power?

Students will also analyse a short excerpt from the U.S. Bill of Rights (Amendments 1, 4, and 6) as primary sources, connecting their analysis directly to our inquiry into how organized systems protect individuals and communities.

"The founders designed a system that makes it hard for any one branch to act alone. They were afraid of what unchecked power looks like, and this week, through history and through the stories we read, students will understand exactly why."
📐
Math — Algebraic Reasoning
Week 1
▾

This week in Math, students will discover that mathematical expressions work like organized systems. The rules must be followed in the correct order, or the meaning changes entirely. All context problems this week are set in 1943 Denmark, making the transdisciplinary connection concrete and meaningful.

🔢
Order of Operations (PEMDAS)
Diagnostic and guided practice. Why does the order matter? Because like any organized system, changing the order changes the result.
🔣
Multi-step Expressions and True/False Equations
Grouping symbols, nested parentheses, and evaluating equations, justifying with evidence, not intuition.
🔤
Translating Word Problems into Expressions
Key vocabulary: the sum of, the product of, decreased by, the quotient of. Precision in mathematical language is a form of clarity.
📊
Patterns and Input/Output Tables
Identifying rules in numerical patterns. A mathematical rule, like a law, predicts what happens next when you follow it consistently. Preview of Grade 6 functions!
📚
ELA — Reading, Writing & Spelling
Number the Stars begins!
▾

📖 Reading

📖

This week we begin reading Number the Stars by Lois Lowry, a powerful historical novel set in Copenhagen, Denmark in 1943. Students will read as investigators, using annotation codes to analyse how Lowry uses setting and character to show, not tell, how occupation shapes daily life. This is not just a story. It is built on true events.

We recommend that students read for about 20 minutes every night at home throughout this unit. It does not need to be the novel we are reading in class, but any book that sparks their curiosity! At the end of the unit, students will write a full book review of one book they read throughout our inquiry, so this is a great time to find a book they truly connect with.

✏️ Writing

This week's writing focus is on ATL Communication skills, reading critically, annotating with purpose, and building the precise academic vocabulary needed to discuss complex historical and literary ideas.

🔤 Spelling — 10 Words from Chapters 1–4

Words are drawn directly from the novel. A great way to practice at home is to ask your child to explain what each word means in the context of the story!

lanky synagogue occupation curfew solemnly obstinate imperious contemptuous exasperate pride
🙏
Worship
Courage · Justice · Community
▾

Worship this week is teacher led and will focus on themes of courage, justice, and community, values woven throughout the stories and ideas at the heart of our new unit.

As we explore histories of occupation, resistance, and solidarity, students will reflect on what it means, in faith and in life, to stand up for what is right, even when it is difficult. These are not abstract ideas. They are the stories of real people who organized themselves around what they believed.

💙
RULER — Emotional Learning
Transition emotions · Meta-Moment
▾

Transitioning from Exhibition into a new unit brings a full range of emotions: pride, nostalgia, relief, curiosity, and the excitement of a fresh start. All of these are welcome and valid.

This week, we'll use the Mood Meter to help students recognize and name these feelings clearly. We'll also revisit the Meta-Moment (pausing before reacting) as students settle into new routines, new collaborative work, and the sometimes deep and challenging stories we'll be exploring.

💬 You can support at home by asking: "What emotion are you feeling about the new unit? What color is it on the Mood Meter?"

📅
Important Dates
▾

  • Wed Apr 22 🌍 Earth Day Celebration! Color Run, Chapel, Family Activities & Market from 2pm. See Earth Day card below.

🌍
Earth Day Celebration — Wed, April 22
Chapel · Color Run · Family Activities · Market
▾
👕Attire: Athletic PE shirt, shorts & athletic shoes. Jeans are OK — no rips. School sports shirts and Feed the Need shirts are welcome too!
🎽Please bring: A change of clothes for after the Color Run. Towel optional.
🕘8:45am — Earth Day Chapel (Auditorium): Our 5th graders will be presenting on the UN Sustainable Development Goals. Come cheer them on!
🎨12:20pm — Color Run (Track & Field): 5th Grade hits the field! Students will run through water and color — hence the change of clothes. 😄
👨‍👩‍👧2:00pm — Family Earth Day Activities & Optional Early Pick-Up at Harrison Hall Loop: DJ, food trucks, booths & vendors (Chick-Fil-A, Freeze Your Brain, True Amor Flowers, and more!). Families are welcome to pick up students early at 2pm if they wish.
🦊
Help Us Help Them — Dress Down Wednesdays
6 Weeks · $5 Donation · Supports Wildlife
▾

Panthers are excited to invite you to join us in supporting a meaningful cause connected to our current line of inquiry, where our learners have been exploring the ways people and animals share spaces.

As part of this learning, our Panthers are taking action by helping raise funds for wildlife through our "Help Us Help Them" dress down initiative. For a $5 donation, students are invited to dress down every Wednesday for the next six weeks in animal prints, nature-inspired outfits, or neutral tones.

All proceeds will go directly to support the Juniper Fox Wildlife Sanctuary, helping us extend our classroom learning into real-world impact. Thank you for partnering with us as we encourage our learners to care for living things and make a difference in the world. 🌿

🌿
3rd Grade Farmers Market — Earth Day!
Apr 22 · 2:30pm · Harrison Car Loop
▾

Our 3rd Grade learners have been working hard creating products as part of their action for their previous unit. Come shop their market on Earth Day, April 22nd at 2:30pm at the Harrison Car Loop!

Cash preferred — card still accepted.

📝
Homework Reminders
▾
Math (Monday): Order of Operations practice sheet goes home on Monday and is due Friday.
Math (Tuesday): Multi-step Expressions practice sheet goes home on Tuesday and is due Friday.
Reading (every night): We recommend 20 minutes of independent reading every night. Students can choose any book they enjoy! At the end of the unit they will write a full book review of one book they read throughout our inquiry, so this is a wonderful opportunity to find a book that truly speaks to them.
Spelling: Words go home Monday. Practice daily throughout the week! Ask your child to use them in sentences or explain what each word means in the context of the story.
💛
Questions or feedback? Reach out anytime — I love hearing from you!
Have a wonderful weekend, and see you Monday for a brand-new beginning! 🌟
